sentences.text: Good afternoon.
My name was Masha, M-A-S-H-A, Frydman, F-R-Y-D-M-A-N.
The 22nd, of the 10th, 1927.
Oh, about 69.
I was born in Sosnowiec, which was quite a big town on the-- so between-- very close to Germany.
It was [POLISH].
Right.
Well, what I remember-- Sosnowiec was a very sort-- a city where there was a lot of Jews.
I think there was 70,000 Jews.
And there was a lot going on, like at one stage I was going even-- there were religious Jews, and there were non-religious Jews.
There were.
I-- I remember-- so like the house where we were living was quite a few very religious Jews.
And although my father was really not a religious man, I had very high regard for him.
And he was friendly with the religious Jews.

Also in the school where I was-- so it was-- the school was called [POLISH], which means that we were not a-- Saturday, we were not going to school, but Sunday, which was very unusual, because the Polish government at that stage was sort of half Fascist.
And for to give allowances like that for Jewish kids was quite unusual.
But that was one of the schools that I was at.
And I grew up with quite a good knowledge of Jewish history.
And we had religious instructions and Jewish history.
And those are the things so that one carries through life.
Like my parents-- you want to know what my parents did and--
Well, the name of my father was Josef Frydman.
The name of my mother was Yachet.
They both came from a small town somewhere-- 100 kilometers from Warsaw.
The town was called Losice.
My father was a leader.
My father was a Bundist, and a leader in the trade union movement.
And he was sent, in 1924, he was sent to Sosnowiec to form a trade union movement.
And this is where they married, and this is where I was born.
Later on, they sent for my maternal mother.
I mean my-- because so in a way she was looking after me all these years.
And she was living with us.
And she had-- because she was a very religious woman, she had a kind of-- whatever I know about religion was her-- it was because of her.
Her name was [POLISH].
And I-- she died, I think, in 1937.
She died before the Holocaust.
But she was living with us, and I remember all the stories that were happening because my father didn't want kosher food.
And she insisted on kosher food, so we should cook always for herself a little pot with kosher food.
And so for us, it was-- we were always very surprised, because she was arguing with him.
And my father was such a strong character that you-- you didn't-- you didn't think that anyone in the world could argue with him.
But she used to be the winner on a lot of occasions.
Yes, I had a sister and a brother.
My brother was Liam.
My brother was two years younger than me.
And my sister was four years younger than me.
And so they went to the same-- to the same kind of schools that I went.
And we were quite a happy family.
I had, apart from the immediate like sisters and brothers, I had an uncle, which was called David-- Actually, he was called [YIDDISH] David.
That was working for my father for many years.
My father had a factory.
Then he was-- at the beginning, he was living with us.
Then he married, and he didn't live with us anymore.
Then I had my father's-- my father's brother, which also was from Losice.
He would also come and work for my father.
You know, it was, my father was a very sort of person that-- that was able to come out in, sort, in business world.
And he had quite a big factory.
It wasn't huge.
It was sort of like a factory, but-- but his immediate family benefited out of him.
Otherwise, they would have to work for somebody else.
And--
He had a shoe factory.
And I even remember I even met in Melbourne people that were dealing with him, because he used to buy some material for the shoes.
And I met people here that knew him well.
But actually, my father came, lived with us in Australia when we came to Melbourne.
But that's for later on.
Well, I remember, as I said, we were all like-- the children were all Jewish children.
I did not come in contact with many non-Jewish children.
It's later on in life, when I came to Russia, that I had only non-Jewish kids.
But in the [POLISH], everybody was Jewish.
And I mean, I really don't remember all that much, because when the war broke out, I was-- so some people remember better.
I was not 12 yet.
And it was just elementary school.
And there were so many-- later on in life, I had so many stories that I don't remember all that well my girlfriends from-- from the school.
But it was a Jewish school, and I never met anyone-- as a matter of fact, I heard later on, that they all died in concentration camps, that they were all in the ghetto in Sosnowiec.
But I thought I haven't met one.
After the war, I didn't meet anybody from-- from my school days.
Yes, I was-- well, this, because my father was almost an atheist, and-- and my grandmother was desperate-- she wanted me to have the Jewish education.
So, not-- she didn't want to tell my father, because she knew she won't succeed.
So she took me-- she sent me to [POLISH] for quite a few years.
And my father was very upset when he learned later on.
But as far as I am concerned, I am very happy now, because I've learned a lot of Judaism, a lot of sort things that I probably would not have learned any other way.
My favorite pastimes-- I am always up to now.
I'm a very good reader.
And I read a lot.
I remember now things.
I read much more than a general sort of-- that a girl my age would read, which went with me through life, and it was very useful.
I also used to help, occasionally, my father.
Because the Jews in Poland-- a lot of Jews in Poland would come from little, small villages or larger villages, and their Polish wasn't first class.
So whatever was necessary to use for some kind of-- well, you had to speak good Polish.
My father would send me.
I remember once a story that my little sister-- I think she was, at that stage, two and a half-- got lost.
And somebody said that they saw a policeman taking her to the police station.
So my-- my father said to me, go and pick up Sala, and-- But don't tell them your name.
And this was later on a standing joke, because everybody was very impressed the way that I came to the police station, and they asked me, what is your name?
And I said Masha.
And they said, the second name.
I said, I haven't got a second name.
And finally, because I-- I couldn't work out but-- later on, my father was scared probably that I will sort charge him with something.
And I go to head out of it.
And so everywhere, whenever they needed things to be done, if it was-- I mean if it included Polish, I would go and do the job.
They were-- yes, although they were not religious, because-- At that stage, belonging to Bund was, in a way-- the Bund was-- I don't want to go into political discussions.
But the Bund tried very hard to make people-- they were socialists, and they were not believers.
But-- but they still were quite traditional, let's say.
So we would have a Seder.
Like I remember before Yom Tov, we'd always go and buy wine and buy macaroons.
And we will-- the children will get new clothes.
And then so later on in life, religious or not, we tried to keep-- to keep it always.
Yes, we had Passover.
I was also very often invited like for-- like some of the neighbors would have Sukkot.
And they would invite the children.
And my grandmother will go to that Sukkot and would look out.
We didn't celebrate every Yom Tov.
But we did celebrate.
So in a way, today you would call it traditional Jews without religion, without going to synagogue, because my father did not go to synagogue.
The main languages that we spoke was Yiddish.
I spoke Polish in school and with my friends.
I-- we also spoke German, because it was very close to the German border, and my father dealt with-- with a lot of folks, Deutsche and Germans.
So German really was the third language.
This was the three languages that I spoke very early in life.
But later on, on top of it came Russian, which I went to Russian schools.
And then French, and now English.
So I speak six, seven languages.
Well, you-- one reads a lot now.
At that stage, I really didn't have very much of a feel, because we didn't come in contact.
Sosnowiec had 70,000 Jews.
And everything was Jewish.
You know, I don't even-- I remember people say that the Poles were anti-Semite.
But I personal, did not come in touch with it.
On contrary, when the war started, we had one of our neighbors, a Polish woman, helped me to buy tickets.
But I'll talk about it later on when we'll talk about the war.
No, I didn't have any unpleasant incidents in all my-- in all those years that I was growing up.
I don't remember anything.
So like I never had any confrontation with anti-Semitic Poles.
Yes.
Yes, my father was-- my father was-- really he was the founder of the trade union.
But this was the Jewish trade union movement.
I don't know much about the Polish trade union movement.
But he was a founder of the Jewish trade union movement, which was, later on, in-- in Australia already, he wrote a book about it.

This was-- there was a lot, you know, like all those myths, the Jews are very wealthy, are really myths, because there was a lot of Jews that were very poor in Sosnowiec, that were working class people.
And those trade union movements, sort accommodated them, did things for them.
I remember meetings.
I remember even going with my father to certain meetings.
Also, I remember going to Bund meetings.
But those are all vague memories.
I don't remember so very much about it.
There was-- no, I didn't attend a Bund youth group for a lot of-- I was too young.
By the time, like, you had to be older, 15, 16.
And I didn't come to that stage.
And there was no Yiddish school.
This I remember my father always being very upset about it, that from-- no matter how many Jews Sosnowiec had-- there was a Hebrew gymnasium, but there were no Jewish schools.
I mean, when I say Jewish schools, I mean Yiddish-speaking schools.
Because he was an Yiddishist.
And Yiddish was to him very important.
And there were no Yiddish-speaking schools.
He always was hoping that one day I would be able to do it.
But unfortunately, the war broke it up all.
Yes.
Well, we were very close to the border.
And we were very-- how can I say-- we were very much aware.
I remember when something that I couldn't, at that stage, understand when-- when the Germans sent out Jews like from [GERMAN].
This was something.
And a lot of Jews-- several thousands of Jews arrived in Sosnowiec.
And we gave even accommodation.
And they were German-speaking Jews, which for us it was a novelty.
You know, so those were-- that was the beginning.
Then we heard rage.
And we were very-- we would listen to Hitler and what Hitler said.
And my father kept on constantly-- as long as I remember-- he kept on saying that if the Germans will come here, we will have to run away.
If the Germans will come-- he was prepared, in his mind, that he was not going to stay with the Germans.
Because we were so close to the border, so we were very concerned.
And we saw those Jews from [GERMAN] that were kicked out, German-Jews-- I don't know other Jews-- that were kicked out from Germany.
And although they were living for years there, they were kicked out.
And they came to all those places in Poland.
So those were the first indication.
And as I said, the wireless and the newspapers, especially for me the wireless.
We constantly heard Hitler speak and what he's going to do and how he's going to do.
And those were-- we were living with it for the last few years before the war, we were full of it.
You mean before the war?
No, I-- I don't remember.
As I said, I wasn't 12.
I was only 11 or something, you know.
And I wasn't concerned.
Even if it was, I don't know much about it.
For me, the first indication-- for me, the first thing, when I saw airplanes and a lot of airplanes.
And I can't even say that I was scared.
On the contrary, I was thinking that something interesting was going to happen.
You know?
And it never, in my mind-- so nobody knew-- anyway, not-- not me.
I-- I didn't think that such a terrible thing can happen.
And I just saw airplanes.
And I saw some action.
So I thought it's going to be interesting.
And that was the beginning.
Like the second day of the war, we had the Germans.

And those are-- this is something that I really will never forget, because what did happen the day the war broke out, my father said to-- to my mother and actual to me that he is going to walk over-- he is going to walk, and he wants us to pack a few things and to go in a different direction.

He said he is going because there were talks already about-- that he always had in mind to walk over to the Russian side, to-- to the Russians, because he felt that this-- this way maybe he'll survive.
So I remember we packed a few things on a little-- I even don't know what you call it-- on a little-- so like it was-- it was a carriage, not a horse an carriage, but just-- and we walked.
And we walked maybe 50 kilometers.
You know, my mother wasn't a great walker.
And we were three little kids.
And I was sort like the main star there.
I was pushing the thing.
And suddenly, in the middle of nowhere, we were 70 kilometers out of Sosnowiec, we were surrounded by the Germans.
But in a very-- you know, it was a field.
And I saw the Germans coming with those big bayonets.
And halt, halt!
You know, it's something for many, many years, if I was dreaming about something terrible, this was that feeling of the Germans.
German uniform, it was German, and surrounding us.
And they didn't do anything.
I mean, they didn't hit us or anything.
They just said back, back, zuruck.
And we went back.
That was-- we came back to Sosnowiec.
Must have been the third day of the war.
My father wasn't home.
A few days later, he also turned up.
And as long, as I said before, from the moment he came back, and even before he started to think and plan how to leave Sosnowiec and to go more east.
But it wasn't an easy thing, because immediately there came out all different rules, like the Jews-- Jews were not allowed to travel, you know.
With every day, Jews were shot in the street.
And every day, new-- new things, which were horrible, which we were very scared-- this was the time when I really started to be very scared.
And then what I am saying are the most important thing.
I don't remember a lot of little, that-- I-- I remember constantly my father saying, we have to move.
We have to go.
We shouldn't be staying with the Germans.
They'll kill us.
I saw Hitler promised, and he's going to do it.
That was what he was constantly saying.
And finally, he worked out a plan that I should go with that my next door neighbor.
And we should buy tickets, because Jews were not allowed to travel.
And my Polish was very good, and I didn't look Jewish.

Because it was already started, the difference between Jewish looks and non-Jewish looks-- things that I was never aware of before, you suddenly started to realize that you shouldn't be-- that it's not good to-- to have a long nose, and it's not good to have brown eyes, you know, because that sort is something that-- that-- that-- So-- so we went.
We bought tickets for-- for the night train.
And all of us, which is without my father, which is my mother and the three kids and Mrs.
Krupska.
This was the Polish woman.
So my mother put on a scarf, so she looked like a peasant woman.
And we traveled.
And it took us a while to come to Warsaw.
We were very scared.
We came to Warsaw.
Warsaw was a horror at that stage.
We had a place-- some very distant relatives.
But Warsaw was completely-- Warsaw was so-- I mean, everybody knows it now.
But it was, almost everything was in ruins.
It was very, very-- Sosnowiec was not, because they came in without completely-- nobody sort had enough time to say a thing.
But in Warsaw, there were fights.
But we were lucky.
We got that family of ours, and we stayed with them a couple of days.
And we organized a horse and carriage.
And it took us a night again, and we came to [POLISH] Losice.
That was the second time in my life that I came there.
I-- I hadn't-- I didn't even remember it.
It was a village.
But later on, I read it was a village 99% Jewish.
Not a little-- it was a shtetl.
It was quite-- so like a village where there was a lot of Jews at that stage.
But this was a point-- [POLISH] Losice was a point.
It was not far.
What I didn't explain that there was a Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.
And in this pact they divided Poland.
The River Bug divided Poland in two parts.
The East belonged-- so the Russian took.
And the West, the Germans took.
And we were in the German occupied zone.
So what we tried very hard-- it took us quite a few months of planning at that-- how do we get to that Russian occupied zone, which was a very hard thing.
We've heard rumors that thousands of people died swimming the river and thousands of people returned back and shot by the Germans.
So we were planning how to make sort-- how to succeed.
So anyway, we were sitting in [POLISH] Losice, where I didn't know many people, but obviously my mother knew.
And we were waiting until my father will be able, because he could not travel anymore already.
So he had to walk.
And he did walk, which was a long, long way.
It took him quite a few weeks, until he came to [POLISH] Losice.
How did he know you were there?
[INTERPOSING VOICES] Did you manage to keep in contact?
No, no, no, no.
No, we did not manage.
We haven't seen.
We haven't heard from him.
We just-- those were the arrangements that we are going to stay there and wait for him, no matter how long it'll take.
And one day he turned up.
And that day was for us really very important, because we realized that maybe something will be done, that maybe we'll be able-- and then they found some smuggler.
It was already winter.
The river was frozen.

And the smuggler promised to take us across the river, which was, I think, seven kilometers-- which was anyway a terrible job, because we were three little kids, and my mother, that was not a very, not a very healthy woman.
And we had to cross a river.
And on one side, the German army.
On the other side, the Russian army.
But we somehow managed.
He got us to the point-- The smuggler got us really to the point where we avoided the Germans.
But in the middle of everything, he left us.
And we were crossing the river, not knowing really if this is the right spot, but we somehow-- In the middle of the river, my brother said he can't walk anymore.
And he set down on the rucksack.
He said, no.
My father started to beg him.
Finally, we managed.
This was the first of January, 1940.
It was a bitter, bitter cold day.
And we managed to cross.
And we came over to the Russian part.
How did we know?
We didn't really know it was the Russian part.
We just, from far away, we heard singing.
We heard Russian songs.
And we thought that it's the Russian part.
And after walking another few kilometers, we came to a little village.
And then we were told that this is the Russian part, which was wonderful for us.
So it meant life.
And this was the first of January, 1940.
Do you remember the name of the village?
Yes.
No, no.
The name of that little village, I don't remember.
But there was a larger village, where we were aiming to go was Siemiatycze.
You know, no.
There were-- you know, there were rumors.
People were saying there were a lot of people crossing in different spots.
I saw now somebody crossed in a different spot.
Thousands of people were crossing.
And I think that my father heard that this is the safest spot.
So we came to Siemiatycze where we stayed maybe a week.
And from there on-- you know, look.
You had to have money.
It's-- it's-- it's-- now, it's easy to say why and what.
We were completely-- all that we had were the rucksacks, you know.
We're three little kids and two people.
And we-- we, sort like-- so from Siemiatycze, we stayed in Siemiatycze a week.
I remember that it was very joyful.
This is something that I do remember.
I remember even for the first time, we were already hungry.
We were starving with-- with the Germans, because they were all different food rations and all that.
And in the little village, there was not much food at all.
So what I remember that my mother bought for the first time a kilo of onions and a bit of oil.
And she fried the onions.
And we had bread and fried onions, you know.
And this was something really that-- it's a thing that I remember, you can imagine, from 1940.
The food situation-- altogether it was an easier-- you know, we felt very happy.
We did live with a family?
We were in one room with a family.
A Jewish Family.
Almost everybody there was Jewish.
Everybody that we came into contact was Jewish.
And they were very helpful.
We're living with a Jewish family, and they were helping my father later on to buy tickets, to change some money.
So we were able to go later on to Lutsk-- we where we were hoping to stay, so like through the war there.
But it didn't eventuate.
Terrific.
That's what I--
